answer:
**Charles Comte's foundational claim** appears in his articulation of liberty as the precondition for all rights, including dominion over the fruits of one's labor, which naturally generates property through free exercise and occupation.[^1] Liberty, for Comte, negates slavery's principle—wherein one man possesses another and seizes labor's products—and affirms instead that "a man can never be legitimately possessed by another, and that the products of his labors belong only to him, so long as he has not freely alienated them."[^2] Property thus emerges as liberty "in the world": the innocent exercise of faculties on unowned things (via occupation) or labor transforms them into exclusive domains essential for human development, as no progress occurs under communalism or slavery.[^3]

**A representative procedural rule from Yājñavalkya's Book 2** (on *vyavahāra*, legal procedure) for disputed property is the hierarchy of proofs: "Proof is declared to be a document, possession, and witnesses. In the absence of any of these, one of the ordeals is prescribed."[^4] Title generally trumps possession unless long-term enjoyment (20 years for land, 10 for movables) without protest establishes it; boundaries are settled by neighbors, elders, cowherds, etc., via marks like mounds or trees.[^5]

**Does this *smṛti* procedure presuppose Comte's liberty?** Partially, but not fundamentally. It presupposes *property* as a baseline—recognizing title, possession, documents, and long enjoyment—which aligns superficially with Comte's view that liberty manifests in appropriating unowned things and labor's fruits.[^6] Disputes imply a right to invoke neutral adjudication (king/assessors per *dharma*), protecting against arbitrary seizure, which Comte would endorse as securing liberty's effects.[^7] However, the system embeds hierarchy Comte abhors: *varṇa*-scaled fines/ordeals, *brāhmaṇa* privileges (e.g., banishment over fines), ordeals for the weak, and slavery's acceptance (e.g., *śūdra* labor).[^8] Liberty for Comte requires equal absence of obstacles to faculties for *all*, rejecting slavery-derived jurisprudence; Yājñavalkya's ritualized, caste-bound process reflects such "ancient" servitude, not pure liberty.[^9]

[^1]: Charles Comte, *Traité de la propriété: VOL I*, [Chapter 3](/works/charles-comte-traite-de-la-propriete-vol-i-2c7a99/03-de-ce-qui-constitue-la-liberte/translation/).

[^2]: Charles Comte, *Traité de la propriété: VOL I*, [Chapter 3](/works/charles-comte-traite-de-la-propriete-vol-i-2c7a99/03-de-ce-qui-constitue-la-liberte/translation/#p-86c544).

[^3]: Charles Comte, *Traité de la propriété: VOL I*, [Chapter 4](/works/charles-comte-traite-de-la-propriete-vol-i-2c7a99/04-de-loccupation-des-choses/translation/#p-8094b8); [Preface](/works/charles-comte-traite-de-la-propriete-vol-i-2c7a99/00-preface/translation/#p-fda115).

[^4]: Yājñavalkya, *Yājñavalkya Smṛti*, [Chapter 2](/works/unknown-yajnavalkya-smrti-cb88d6/02/translation/#p-e6e133).

[^5]: Yājñavalkya, *Yājñavalkya Smṛti*, [Chapter 2](/works/unknown-yajnavalkya-smrti-cb88d6/02/translation/#p-e9236b); [Chapter 2](/works/unknown-yajnavalkya-smrti-cb88d6/02/translation/#p-382005).

[^6]: Charles Comte, *Traité de la propriété: VOL I*, [Chapter 4](/works/charles-comte-traite-de-la-propriete-vol-i-2c7a99/04-de-loccupation-des-choses/translation/#p-41a64c).

[^7]: Charles Comte, *Traité de la propriété: VOL I*, [Chapter 3](/works/charles-comte-traite-de-la-propriete-vol-i-2c7a99/03-de-ce-qui-constitue-la-liberte/translation/#p-360eea).

[^8]: Yājñavalkya, *Yājñavalkya Smṛti*, [Chapter 2](/works/unknown-yajnavalkya-smrti-cb88d6/02/translation/#p-37e7f9); [Chapter 2](/works/unknown-yajnavalkya-smrti-cb88d6/02/translation/#p-f5049f) (witness exclusions by caste); [Chapter 2](/works/unknown-yajnavalkya-smrti-cb88d6/02/translation/#p-18382).

[^9]: Charles Comte, *Traité de la propriété: VOL I*, [Chapter 1](/works/charles-comte-traite-de-la-propriete-vol-i-2c7a99/01-chapitre-premier-de-linfluence-des-doctrines-des-p/translation/) (not read, but per metadata/work description: critiques "ancient jurisprudence grounded in slavery").
